metadata.summary: Framework for AI risk scoring, schedule variance, capacity forecasting, and stakeholder digests. AI boosts individual velocity but organizational throughput bottlenecks shift to review/deploy.

content.title: AI in Project Management
content.summary: Framework for applying AI to project risk scoring, schedule variance analysis, resource capacity forecasting, and stakeholder digest generation. Anchor: PMBOK 8 AI Appendix and DORA 2025 Productivity Paradox — AI boosts individual task velocity but organizational throughput stays flat until bottlenecks in review/deploy are addressed.

why.p: AI PM tools adoption reached 47-54% across risk, task automation, and scheduling (PMI 2024). The integration pattern that works: AI as observation and synthesis layer (data aggregation, anomaly surfacing, report generation) with human-initiated action triggers. The pattern that fails: AI as autonomous decision layer for budget reallocations and deadline changes without audit trail.

list.item: Risk register needs automated scoring across sprint data (staleness, dependent-task count, owner response rate).
Stakeholder reports require synthesis from multiple sources (Jira, GitHub, budget sheets).
Schedule variance must run continuously, not just at milestones.
Resource allocation decisions need capacity forecasting across 3+ team members.
Post-mortems need pattern extraction from historical project data.
Project risk register needs automated scoring and trend analysis across sprint data.
Projects with fewer than 3 people: overhead exceeds benefit.
Decisions carrying legal/contractual weight: require full human sign-off with audit trail.
Teams without baseline PM tooling (Jira/Linear/GitHub): AI has nothing to feed on.
Highly political one-off decisions where stakeholder dynamics dominate data signals.
Project has fewer than 3 people — overhead exceeds benefit.
Decisions carry legal or contractual weight — AI recommendations need full human sign-off with audit trail.
Team lacks baseline PM tooling (Jira/Linear/GitHub) — AI has nothing to feed on.

section.name: AI Adoption by PM Area
section.p: Risk management (predictive risk identification, probability forecasting): 54% adoption.
Task automation (routine task handling, workflow automation): 53% adoption.
Predictive analysis (outcome forecasting, trend detection): 52% adoption.
Schedule optimization (timeline prediction, dependency analysis): 52% adoption.
Resource planning (capacity forecasting, skill matching): 47% adoption.

section.name: The AI Productivity Paradox

rule.statement: After introducing AI coding tools, measure organizational delivery throughput — not just individual task velocity — and use Value Stream Mapping to locate the shifted bottlenecks.
rule.rationale: DORA 2025 shows AI coding assistants boost individual output (21% more tasks, 98% more PRs) but organizational delivery metrics stay flat. Bottlenecks shift to review, integration, and deployment. Ignoring this leads to local optimization while system throughput stagnates.

section.p: Practical implication: AI-boosted dev velocity increases the queue at code review and deployment. The PM role shifts to surfacing these downstream bottlenecks, not celebrating velocity numbers.
The AI Productivity Paradox applies: AI boosts individual task velocity, but organizational throughput bottlenecks shift to review and deployment — flat delivery metrics persist.

section.name: PMBOK 8 AI Integration Approach
section.p: First dedicated AI coverage in PMBOK 8. Addresses ethical technology use, AI-assisted decisions, and sustainable project delivery.

list.item: Start with low-risk, high-value AI use cases.
Maintain human oversight for all decisions.
Validate AI recommendations against source data.
Document AI-assisted decisions with model version, input hash, reviewer name.
Build team AI literacy before tool adoption.
Monitor for shifted bottlenecks post-adoption.

section.name: Industry Benchmarks
section.p: 96% prediction accuracy: Intelligent Project Prediction tool (requires clean structured data pipelines — most teams do not have them).
15% reduction in budget overruns: Acciona (construction, AI schedule forecasting).
84% improved efficiency: Project.co survey (broad self-reported, not controlled).

rule.statement: Validate AI schedule predictions against actuals for at least 3 sprints before including them in stakeholder reports.
rule.rationale: 96% accuracy claims assume input data quality that most teams do not have. 3-sprint calibration surfaces systematic bias before the report reaches executives.

section.name: Agentic Architecture
section.p: A Claude subagent is well-suited to the data-aggregation and synthesis layer: pull structured data from project APIs, run heuristic risk scoring, generate stakeholder digests, surface anomalies. Never let agents trigger budget reallocations or deadline changes autonomously — gate all writes behind a human confirmation step.

rule.statement: Use Haiku for routine weekly digest generation; use Sonnet for retrospective pattern analysis across multiple sprints.
rule.rationale: Weekly digests are pattern application on structured JSON — low reasoning demand. Retrospective pattern analysis requires cross-sprint reasoning about causes and trends.

rule.statement: Scope AI automation to observation and synthesis; keep action triggers human-initiated.
rule.rationale: An agent that auto-closes risks or auto-updates status fields can corrupt the PM system state. The PM tool is the source of truth; agent writes without human approval destroy auditability.

section.name: Risk Scoring and Analysis
section.p: AI risk scoring is only as good as the input data; stale or missing issue updates degrade accuracy significantly.

rule.statement: Log the record count fetched versus expected from Jira/Linear on every API call — rate limits can silently truncate data.
rule.rationale: Silent truncation gives a partial sprint view that looks complete. Risk scores and velocity calculations built on partial data produce false confidence.

rule.statement: Timestamp-gate sprint data queries: if the agent runs mid-sprint, partial data skews velocity calculations.
rule.rationale: Sprint boundary detection requires knowing whether the sprint has ended. Running on day 3 of 14 gives data that cannot be compared to completed-sprint baselines.

section.name: Limitations and Gotchas
section.p: Schedule predictions drift when external dependencies (vendors, clients) are not tracked in the PM tool.
AI-generated summaries can obscure nuance — a "green" risk status may hide a critical qualitative flag only a human catches.
DORA 2025 shows 96% prediction accuracy requires clean, structured data pipelines — most teams do not have them.

antipattern.description: LLM hallucinating task IDs and dates in long-context report generation.
antipattern.reason: Always validate task IDs and dates against the source PM system before including in reports; hallucinated IDs cause stakeholder confusion and erode trust.

antipattern.description: AI-generated summaries reporting "green" status that hides a critical qualitative flag.
antipattern.reason: AI risk scoring is only as good as its structured inputs; qualitative concerns from team members (unstated in tickets) do not appear in data-driven summaries.

antipattern.description: Pinning to no model version for digest agents.
antipattern.reason: A Haiku upgrade can change summary tone noticeably — pin the model; digest style should be consistent across reports.

list.item: Feed AI only structured, timestamped data — free-text PM notes produce low-quality signals.
Maintain a human decision log separate from AI recommendations; regulatory audits require it.
Run AI risk scoring weekly, not per-commit — daily runs amplify noise.
Validate AI schedule predictions against actuals for at least 3 sprints before trusting them in stakeholder reports.
Use Value Stream Mapping alongside AI tooling to locate post-AI bottlenecks (review, deploy) — the productivity paradox is real.
Scope AI automation to observation and synthesis; keep action triggers human-initiated.
Document each AI-assisted decision with the model version, input data hash, and reviewer name.

section.name: Recommended Subagents
section.p: `faion-sdd-executor-agent` — executes structured SDD tasks including implementation plans; can synthesize task-level progress into a sprint summary.
Custom PM-digest agent — polls Jira/Linear API, computes schedule variance, generates Markdown report; runs on cron.

template.purpose: Project status digest prompt template for sprint JSON data
Python script: score risk items Low/Medium/High from JSON risk register

list.item: `jira-cli` — Query Jira issues, sprints, boards from terminal. Install: brew install ankitpokhrel/tap/jira-cli. Docs: github.com/ankitpokhrel/jira-cli
`gh` (GitHub CLI) — Pull PR/issue data into agent context. Install: brew install gh. Docs: cli.github.com
`linear` CLI — Linear issue queries (no official CLI; use GraphQL API). Docs: linear.app/docs/graphql
`gsheet-cli` — Read/write Google Sheets for budget tracking. Docs: github.com/lidaobing/gsheet-cli
`mlflow` CLI — Track ML experiment runs if AI models are embedded in delivery pipeline. Install: pip install mlflow
Jira (SaaS) — Rich sprint/issue data; webhook support for real-time triggers. REST API v3. Fully agent-friendly.
Linear (SaaS) — Cleaner API than Jira; good for agent polling. GraphQL API. Fully agent-friendly.
GitHub Projects (SaaS) — Useful when dev and PM tracking are unified. GraphQL API. Fully agent-friendly.
Notion (SaaS) — Good for document synthesis; limited structured query. REST API. Partial agent-friendly support.
Microsoft Project (SaaS/Desktop) — Useful if org is MS-centric. Graph API. Partial agent-friendly support (complex auth).
ProjectManager.com (SaaS) — Predictive analytics built-in; agent can augment. REST API. Fully agent-friendly.
Forecast.app (SaaS) — AI-native PM tool; resource forecasting endpoint. REST API. Fully agent-friendly.
Clockify (SaaS) — Time-tracking data feeds resource capacity models. REST API. Fully agent-friendly.

section.name: Prompt Patterns
section.p: Project status analyst prompt:
section.code:
You are a project status analyst. Given the following sprint data (JSON): <sprint_data>{{sprint_data}}</sprint_data>
1. Identify tasks that are behind schedule by more than 20%. 2. Flag any risks that appeared in the last 7 days. 3. Summarize in 200 words or fewer for a non-technical stakeholder. Do not recommend actions — list findings only. A human will decide next steps. Mark any inferences as "data signal, requires human validation".
section.p: Risk scoring prompt:
section.code: Analyze this risk register and score each item Low/Medium/High based on: - Days since last update - Number of dependent tasks - Owner response rate Return JSON: [{id, score, reason}]

section.name: Inline Risk Scoring Script
